title: Madeleines
subtitle: Shell-shaped French cakes with a lemon and brown butter crumb
excerpt: Madeleines are small French shell-shaped cakes baked in a ribbed tin, made from a genoise-style batter with melted brown butter and lemon zest. The characteristic hump on the back forms when cold batter meets a hot tin: an essential rest of at least 1 hour in the fridge is the trick. They are at their best within an hour of baking.
sourceNotes: Madeleines are a traditional French small cake from the Lorraine region, documented in French domestic cookery from at least the eighteenth century. The origin is disputed but the formula is firmly public domain. The standard recipe uses browned butter (beurre noisette), lemon zest, and a cold-rest technique to produce the characteristic hump.

recipe.batchNotes: The batter keeps in the fridge for 24 hours; bake in batches from one batch of batter.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The batter must rest for at least 1 hour; up to 24 hours is fine and improves the hump.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; the high initial temperature creates the hump

content.text: Madeleines are small, shell-shaped French cakes with a light crumb and a brown butter flavour. The hump on the flat side forms when cold batter hits a very hot tin. The batter must rest in the fridge for at least an hour before baking; that cold rest is what creates the hump.
Brown the butter and make the batter

Melt the butter in a small saucepan over medium heat. Continue cooking, stirring occasionally, until the milk solids at the bottom turn nut-brown and the butter smells of toasted hazelnut, about 4 to 5 minutes. Pour immediately into a cool bowl to stop the cooking. Leave to cool until barely warm. In a separate bowl, whisk the eggs, sugar, honey, vanilla, and lemon zest together until pale and slightly increased in volume. Sift in the flour and baking powder, fold in, then fold in the cooled brown butter. Cover the batter and refrigerate for at least 1 hour.

Heat the oven to 200°C fan. Butter and flour the madeleine tin generously; the ribbed shell pattern only shows if the tin is well prepared. Spoon or pipe the cold batter into the moulds, filling each about three-quarters full. Bake for 11 to 12 minutes. The madeleines are done when the edges are golden and have pulled away from the tin and the hump in the centre has set. Turn out immediately onto a wire rack and eat while still warm.
